title: Some geometric mechanisms for Arnold diffusion
speaker: Rafael de la Llave
abstract: Abstract: We consider the problem whether small perturbations of integrable mechanical systems can have very large effects. It is known that in many cases, the effects of the perturbations average out, but there are exceptional cases (resonances) where the perturbations do accumulate. It is a complicated problem whether this can keep on happening because once the instability accumulates, the system moves out of resonance. V. Arnold discovered in 1964 some geometric structures that lead to accumulation in carefuly constructed examples.

proof_stages.label: Motivation and methodological stance
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames Arnold diffusion as structured instability in nearly integrable Hamiltonian systems and chooses a concrete-system, C1-open, computationally verifiable geometric approach rather than a KAM/twist/genericity approach.

proof_stages.label: Normally hyperbolic invariant manifolds as the skeleton
proof_stages.summary: The speaker recalls the definition and persistence package for normally hyperbolic invariant manifolds, including spectral-gap splittings, stable and unstable manifolds, invariant foliations, parameter dependence, and computability.

proof_stages.label: Homoclinic channels and scattering maps
proof_stages.summary: Transverse intersections of the stable and unstable manifolds define homoclinic channels, wave maps, and lower-dimensional scattering maps on the NHIM, with multiple persistent channels and useful smooth/symplectic properties.

proof_stages.label: Shadowing of multi-channel scattering itineraries
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem states that any pseudo-itinerary obtained by alternating sufficiently long inner iterates with chosen scattering maps is shadowed, within a prescribed tolerance, by a true orbit of the original map.

proof_stages.label: Verification and concrete diffusion consequences
proof_stages.summary: The existence of the required transverse channels can be checked by explicit perturbative or computer-assisted calculations, yielding diffusion results in celestial-mechanics and a priori unstable Hamiltonian settings under nonvanishing/open conditions.

proof_stages.label: Covering-window proof mechanism
proof_stages.summary: A proof sketch builds boxes/windows near successive channel hits and uses hyperbolic expansion/contraction plus topological covering arguments of Conley/Easton type to obtain an orbit tracking all windows, with extra care in center directions.

proof_stages.label: Further controllability and symbolic extensions
proof_stages.summary: The talk closes by pointing to refinements using Lie-bracket controllability of near-identity scattering maps, normally hyperbolic laminations producing symbolic families of channels, and possible PDE/breather applications.

examples.name: Accelerators versus low-energy spacecraft maneuvers
examples.note: Plasma accelerators illustrate instability as an engineering danger, while spacecraft transfers illustrate the same phenomenon as a useful way to move far with little effort.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker only contrasts accelerators, where instability is undesirable, with spacecraft maneuvers, where instability can help travel cheaply, without computing or analyzing either example concretely

passes.reason: Contrasting application settings illustrating that instability can be harmful in accelerators but useful for spacecraft, not a structurally special mathematical object or a refutation.

The transcript uses accelerators and low-energy spacecraft maneuvers only as motivating application domains for stability/instability, not as a specific mathematical object with classifiable structure.
